key_insight: Machine-learning-enhanced path-integral simulations show that nuclear quantum effects dramatically alter hydrogen and deuterium diffusion through layered MoS2, lowering barriers and enabling strong isotope-dependent transport.
engineering_relevance: Accurate prediction of hydrogen permeation in 2D materials can guide the design of membranes and layered structures for hydrogen storage, fuel cells, and isotope separation.
method: Interatomic potentials trained on r2SCAN+rVV10 DFT data
Well-tempered metadynamics combined with path-integral molecular dynamics
Evaluated hydrogen and deuterium diffusion across MoS2 polytypes and twisted bilayers
evidence_signal: The abstract reports that NQEs substantially reduce free-energy barriers at 300 K, increase the hydrogen self-diffusion coefficient versus classical simulations, and reveal a 35 meV H/D quantum barrier difference.
watch_item: Results depend on the quality and transferability of the learned potential and on the sampling adequacy of complex twisted-bilayer free-energy landscapes.
paper_title: Capturing Nuclear Quantum Effects in Hydrogen Diffusion through MoS2 via Machine-Learning-Enhanced Path-Integral Simulations
